{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}} '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakahiko|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Tsunokimi Iwai]] to [[Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]]
revision.text:
{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}} '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakahiko|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

revision.text:
{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakahiko|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]][[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:kk]][[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakahiko|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]] [[ja:{{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

revision.text:
{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakahiko|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Link incomplete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}}[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsunokimi Iwai}}[[Category:pp]][[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[Category:pp]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:Tsunokimi Iwai]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]]

revision.text:
{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles {{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles {{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles ([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles {{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles revision.comment: Adding [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:前方後円墳]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{ill|Chikushi Kimi Iwai|ja|筑紫君磐井}} {{Infobox Japanese noble | Name = Iwai | Image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a [[Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Kuni no Miyatsuko]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Link complete]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記}} describes Iwai's burial mound and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles
