{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=draft}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|12=draft}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|12=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|12=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:S11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}}

The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}}

revision.comment: Bot: single‑shot reformat + ill→|12=draft + draft categories + iws + DEFAULTSORT + move HTML comments + meta‑cat check
revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=draft|12=draft}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|12=draft|12=draft}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft|12=draft}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|12=draft|12=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=draft|12=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|12=draft|12=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|12=draft|12=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}}

Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|12=draft|12=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=draft|12=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|12=draft|12=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}}

{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft|12=draft}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft|12=draft}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=draft|12=draft}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|12=draft|12=draft}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|12=draft|12=draft}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|12=draft|12=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=draft|12=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=draft|12=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|12=draft|12=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|12=draft|12=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}}

revision.comment: 68 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:磐井 (古代豪族) up to rev 101381485
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 磐井 | 画像 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]後期 | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = [[継体天皇]]22年（[[528年]]?） | 改名 = | 別名 = 筑紫岩井、竺紫君石井、筑紫君磐井 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = [[福岡県]][[八女市]]吉田の[[岩戸山古墳]]（{{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=岩戸山古墳}}） | 官位 = [[筑紫国造]]？ | 主君 = | 氏族 = [[筑紫国造]]？ | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[筑紫葛子]] | 特記事項 = }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{Center|[[岩戸山古墳]]（[[福岡県]][[八女市]]）と石製品群}}{{Small|磐井の墓に比定される。北部九州では最大規模。}}]] '''磐井'''（いわい、[[生年不明]] - [[継体天皇]]22年（[[528年]]?））または'''筑紫 磐井'''（つくし/ちくし の いわい）は、[[6世紀]]前半（[[古墳時代]]後期）の豪族。[[カバネ]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「磐井」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''竺紫君石井'''（ちくしのきみ いわい）」、『筑後国風土記』逸文では「'''筑紫君磐井'''」と表記される。『日本書紀』では磐井の官職を[[筑紫国造]]としているが、これを後世の潤色とする説がある{{Sfn|磐井の乱（古代史）|2006年}}{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 [[ヤマト王権]]との間で起こった'''[[磐井の乱]]'''で知られるほか、この時代では珍しく墓の特定が可能な人物として知られる。 == 記録 == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{Center|[[磐井の乱]]関係地}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=筑後 上妻県
（[[岩戸山古墳]]）}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=筑紫 [[三井郡|御井郡]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=糟屋屯倉}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=豊前 上膳県}} }} === 日本書紀 === 『[[日本書紀]]』[[継体天皇]]21年（[[527年]]?）継体天皇晩年の編年は、『百済本記』の伝える辛亥の変（[[継体・欽明朝の内乱]]）により3年繰り上げられたとする説がある。その場合、書紀の527年から528年という紀年は、実際には530年から531年の出来事になる {{Harv|磐井の乱（古代史）|2006年}}。6月3日条によると、[[近江毛野]]が軍6万人を率い、[[任那]]に渡って[[新羅]]に奪われた南加羅・喙己呑（とくことん）を再興して任那を合併しようとした。これに対して、筑紫君磐井が反逆を謀って実行する時をうかがっていると、それを知った新羅から賄賂とともに毛野の軍勢阻止を勧められた。そこで磐井は[[火国]]（後の[[肥前国]]・[[肥後国]]）と[[豊国]]（後の[[豊前国]]・[[豊後国]]）を抑えて海路を遮断し、また[[高句麗]]・[[百済]]・新羅・任那の朝貢船を誘致した。そしてついに毛野軍と戦いになり、その渡航を遮ったという{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}『[[日本古典文学全集|新編日本古典文学全集]] 3 日本書紀 2』小学館、2004年（[[ジャパンナレッジ]]版）、pp. 309-313。。 継体天皇22年（[[528年]]?）11月11日条によると、磐井は[[筑紫国|筑紫]]御井郡（現在の[[福岡県]][[三井郡]]の大部分と[[久留米市]]中央部{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}）において、朝廷から征討のため派遣された[[物部麁鹿火]]の軍と交戦したが、激しい戦いの末に麁鹿火に斬られた。そして同年12月、磐井の子の[[筑紫葛子|筑紫君葛子]]は死罪を免れるため[[糟屋屯倉]]（現・福岡県[[糟屋郡]]・[[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}）を朝廷に献じたという{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 === 古事記 === 『[[古事記]]』では継体天皇段において、竺紫君石井（磐井に同じ）が天皇の命に従わず無礼が多いため、物部荒甲大連（物部麁鹿火）・[[大伴金村|大伴金村連]]の2人が遣わされて石井を殺した、と事件について簡潔に触れている{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 === 風土記 === 『筑後国風土記』逸文（『[[釈日本紀]]』所引）によると、上妻県（かみつやめのあがた：現在の福岡県[[八女郡]]東北部{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}）の役所の南2里（約1キロメートル）に筑紫君磐井の墓があるとする。その墓について詳述した後で古老の伝えとして、雄大迹天皇（継体天皇）の御世に磐井は強い勢力を有して生前に墓を作ったが、俄に官軍が進発し攻めようとしたため、勝ち目のないことを悟って[[豊前国]]上膳県（[[上毛郡]]：現在の福岡県[[築上郡]]南部）へ逃げて身を隠した。そしてこれに怒った官軍は石人・石馬を壊したという{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}『[[日本古典文学全集|新編日本古典文学全集]] 5 風土記』小学館、2003年（[[ジャパンナレッジ]]版）、pp. 309-313。。 === その他 === 『[[先代旧事本紀]]』「[[国造本紀]]」伊吉島造（[[壱岐国造]]）条では、継体天皇の時に石井（磐井）に従った新羅の海辺の人を討伐したとする記述がある。 == 墓 == [[File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|200px|thumb|{{center|石馬（岩戸山古墳出土）}}{{small|岩戸山歴史資料館展示。}}]] 磐井の墓は、『筑後国風土記』逸文に詳述されている。これによれば、墓は高さ7丈（約20メートル）、周囲60丈（約180メートル）で、墓域は南辺・北辺各60丈、東辺・西辺各40丈（約120メートル）。また石人・石盾各60枚があり、交互に陣をなして墓の周囲に巡らされた。さらに東北の角には「衙頭（がとう）」と称する別区を設け、衙頭の中には「[[解部]]（ときべ）」という悠然と立つ人物1人と、裸体で大地に伏す「偸人（ぬすびと）」があった。これは生きていた際に盗人が猪を盗んだため、その罪を受けている様子を表したという。その側には「賊物（かすみもの）」と称する盗んだ石猪4頭とともに、石馬3疋、石殿3軒、石蔵2軒があったと伝えている。 以上の記述に見える墓は、現在では[[福岡県]][[八女市]]吉田の'''[[岩戸山古墳]]'''（{{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=岩戸山古墳}}）に比定される{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。この岩戸山古墳は墳丘長135メートルの[[前方後円墳]]で、北部九州では最大、かつ当時の畿内大王墓にも匹敵する規模の古墳になる{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。その築造年代は6世紀前半と推定され『日本書紀』の年代と一致し、また[[石人石馬|石人・石馬]]を含む多くの石製品が出土し、古墳東北隅には別区の存在も確認され、多くの点で『筑後国風土記』逸文とも一致を見せている{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。なお岩戸山古墳北方約1.1キロメートルの正恵大坪遺跡（[[八女郡]][[広川町 (福岡県)|広川町]]古賀）では、上妻郡衙と見られる遺構が見つかっており、これも『筑後国風土記』逸文に見える上妻県役所の後身に対応するものとして注目されている{{Sfn|大塚恵治|2014年|pp=104-107}}。 岩戸山古墳の位置する八女丘陵では、前方後円墳12基（岩戸山古墳含む）・[[装飾古墳]]3基を含む古墳約300基からなる[[八女古墳群]]が分布する{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=9}}。その築造は[[4世紀]]前半から7世紀前半に及び、筑紫君一族の墓に相当すると推定されている{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=9}}。中でも岩戸山古墳の2世代前にあたる[[石人山古墳]]（八女郡広川町一条）は磐井の祖父の墓と推定されるほか{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=14}}、岩戸山古墳次世代の[[乗場古墳]]（八女市吉田）・[[善蔵塚古墳]]（八女郡広川町六田）・[[鶴見山古墳]]（八女市豊福）のいずれかは子の葛子の墓と推定されている{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=24}}。なお、昭和中頃まではそのうちの石人山古墳を磐井の墓とする説が有力視されていた{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。 == 考証 == {{see also|[[磐井の乱]]}} 磐井の墓と推定される岩戸山古墳は、当時の大王墓にも匹敵する規模であり、また出土した石製品群は北部九州の中でも他古墳を圧倒する数で、北部九州一帯に及んだ磐井の勢力を物語っている{{Sfn|大塚恵治|2014年|pp=104-107}}。その石製品群の分布状況から推測される勢力範囲は、北は[[玄界灘]]、南は[[有明海]]に及んでおり、筑紫君を中心として「筑紫政権」とも呼ぶべき強力な連合政権を形成していたといわれる{{Sfn|角川日本地名大辞典|1988年|p=31}}{{Sfn|大塚恵治|2014年|pp=104-107}}。 近年では、領域の中でも特に有明海沿岸地域が5世紀後半から6世紀初頭にかけての対朝鮮交渉の中心地であったことから、その対朝鮮の外交権を巡って磐井とヤマト王権側との間に対立が生じたとする説が挙げられている{{Sfn|磐井の乱（日本大百科）}}{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2013年|p=196}}。その例として、朝鮮半島の[[栄山江]]流域に分布する[[長鼓墳|前方後円形古墳]]の存在や、栄山江流域・慶南地方に分布する九州系横穴式石室の存在があり、これら九州系豪族が独自に朝鮮半島と密接な交渉を行なった様子が指摘される{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=73-84}}。また日本列島内においても、真の継体天皇陵と目される[[今城塚古墳]]（[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]）を始めとする畿内古墳の石棺部材に[[阿蘇ピンク石]]（馬門ピンク石）が見られることから、九州から西日本・畿内へ文化を波及させるだけの力を有したとされる{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=60-72}}（阿蘇ピンク石の畿内流入は530年頃で終息{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}）。これらから、畿内側が看過できないまで九州勢力が成長していたことが乱の背景になったと考えられている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=73-84}}（詳しくは「[[磐井の乱]]」参照）。 文献の記述に関しては、『筑後国風土記』逸文に記される別区での裁判のような様から、磐井が朝廷において[[解部]]として訴訟に携わったことを表すとする説がある{{Sfn|筑紫磐井（国史）}}。また『日本書紀』における近江毛野との会話からも、やはり磐井が若い頃に王権に出仕した可能性が指摘される{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=60-72}}。ただしこの近江毛野の伝承に関しては、磐井の乱における毛野の動向が記されないことから、磐井の乱と近江毛野の任那派遣とは元々別伝承であったと見られている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}。 なお、史書では子の筑紫君葛子の後も[[7世紀]]末まで筑紫君（筑紫氏）一族の名が見られ、その活躍が認められている{{Sfn|筑紫君葛子（古代氏族）|2010年}}{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=13}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''出典''' {{reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * 百科事典 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=|chapter=|title=[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]]|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=[[平野邦雄]] 「磐井の乱」|ref={{Harvid|磐井の乱（国史）}}}}、{{Wikicite|reference=平野邦雄 「筑紫磐井」|ref={{Harvid|筑紫磐井（国史）}}}}。 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=小林敏男|authorlink=小林敏男 (歴史学者)|year=|chapter=磐井の乱|title=[[日本大百科全書]]（ニッポニカ）|publisher=[[小学館]]|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|磐井の乱（日本大百科）}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=山尾幸久|authorlink=山尾幸久|year=2006|chapter=磐井の乱|title=日本古代史大辞典|publisher=[[大和書房]]|isbn=978-4479840657|ref={{Harvid|磐井の乱（古代史）|2006年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2010|chapter=|title=日本古代氏族人名辞典 普及版|publisher=[[吉川弘文館]]|isbn=9784642014588|ref=}} *** {{Wikicite|reference=「磐井」|ref={{Harvid|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}、{{Wikicite|reference=「筑紫君葛子」|ref={{Harvid|筑紫君葛子（古代氏族）|2010年}}}}。 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=1988|chapter=|title=[[角川日本地名大辞典]] 40 福岡県|publisher=[[角川書店]]|isbn=978-4040014005|ref={{Harvid|角川日本地名大辞典|1988年}}}} * その他書籍 ** 鬼頭清明 「大王と有力豪族 -磐井の戦争とは-」『週刊朝日百科 日本の歴史41 邪馬台国と大王の時代』 朝日新聞社、1987年。 ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=|year=2009|chapter=|title=岩戸山歴史資料館 展示図録|publisher=八女市教育委員会|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=アクロス福岡文化誌編纂委員会|author=|year=2009|chapter=|title=アクロス福岡文化誌 3 古代の福岡|publisher=[[海鳥社]]|isbn=978-4874157138|ref={{Harvid|古代の福岡|2009年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=水谷千秋|authorlink=水谷千秋|year=2013|chapter=|title=継体天皇と朝鮮半島の謎（[[文春新書]]925）|publisher=[[文藝春秋]]|isbn=978-4166609253|ref={{Harvid|水谷千秋|2013年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=河野一隆|year=2013|chapter=磐井の乱|title=週刊新発見! 日本の歴史 第9号 -古墳時代2 ヤマト王権誕生の実態-|publisher=朝日新聞出版|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|河野一隆|2013年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=大塚恵治|year=2014|chapter=岩戸山古墳と筑紫の政権|title=[[歴史読本]] 2015年1月号 -古代王権と古墳の謎-|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=|pages=104-107|ref={{Harvid|大塚恵治|2014年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=柳沢一男|year=2014|chapter=|title=筑紫君磐井と「磐井の乱」 岩戸山古墳（シリーズ「遺跡を学ぶ」094）|publisher=新泉社|isbn=978-4787713346|ref={{Harvid|柳沢一男|2014年}}}} ** {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=水谷千秋|authorlink=水谷千秋|year=2015|chapter=継体天皇の戦い|title=[[歴史読本]] 2015年春号 -争乱の古代史 古代日本国家成立への戦い-|publisher=[[KADOKAWA]]|isbn=|pages=76-83|ref={{Harvid|水谷千秋|2015年}}}} == 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[磐井の乱]] * [[岩戸山古墳]] * [[筑紫薩夜麻]]－[[天智天皇]]期の人物で磐井の子孫と推測される人物。 {{DEFAULTSORT:いわい こたいこうそく}} [[Category:筑紫君磐井|*]] [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:古墳時代以前に戦死した人物]] revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[磐井 (古代豪族)]] to [[Tsukushi no Iwai]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 磐井 | 画像 = | 時代 = [[古墳時代]]後期 | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = [[継体天皇]]22年（[[528年]]?） | 改名 = | 別名 = 筑紫岩井、竺紫君石井、筑紫君磐井 | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = [[福岡県]][[八女市]]吉田の[[岩戸山古墳]]（{{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=岩戸山古墳}}） | 官位 = [[筑紫国造]]？ | 主君 = | 氏族 = [[筑紫国造]]？ | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[筑紫葛子]] | 特記事項 = }} [[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{Center|[[岩戸山古墳]]（[[福岡県]][[八女市]]）と石製品群}}{{Small|磐井の墓に比定される。北部九州では最大規模。}}]] '''磐井'''（いわい、[[生年不明]] - [[継体天皇]]22年（[[528年]]?））または'''筑紫 磐井'''（つくし/ちくし の いわい）は、[[6世紀]]前半（[[古墳時代]]後期）の豪族。[[カバネ]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]。 『[[日本書紀]]』では「磐井」、『[[古事記]]』では「'''竺紫君石井'''（ちくしのきみ いわい）」、『筑後国風土記』逸文では「'''筑紫君磐井'''」と表記される。『日本書紀』では磐井の官職を[[筑紫国造]]としているが、これを後世の潤色とする説がある{{Sfn|磐井の乱（古代史）|2006年}}{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 [[ヤマト王権]]との間で起こった'''[[磐井の乱]]'''で知られるほか、この時代では珍しく墓の特定が可能な人物として知られる。 == 記録 == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{Center|[[磐井の乱]]関係地}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=筑後 上妻県
（[[岩戸山古墳]]）}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=筑紫 [[三井郡|御井郡]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=糟屋屯倉}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=豊前 上膳県}} }} === 日本書紀 === 『[[日本書紀]]』[[継体天皇]]21年（[[527年]]?）継体天皇晩年の編年は、『百済本記』の伝える辛亥の変（[[継体・欽明朝の内乱]]）により3年繰り上げられたとする説がある。その場合、書紀の527年から528年という紀年は、実際には530年から531年の出来事になる {{Harv|磐井の乱（古代史）|2006年}}。6月3日条によると、[[近江毛野]]が軍6万人を率い、[[任那]]に渡って[[新羅]]に奪われた南加羅・喙己呑（とくことん）を再興して任那を合併しようとした。これに対して、筑紫君磐井が反逆を謀って実行する時をうかがっていると、それを知った新羅から賄賂とともに毛野の軍勢阻止を勧められた。そこで磐井は[[火国]]（後の[[肥前国]]・[[肥後国]]）と[[豊国]]（後の[[豊前国]]・[[豊後国]]）を抑えて海路を遮断し、また[[高句麗]]・[[百済]]・新羅・任那の朝貢船を誘致した。そしてついに毛野軍と戦いになり、その渡航を遮ったという{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}『[[日本古典文学全集|新編日本古典文学全集]] 3 日本書紀 2』小学館、2004年（[[ジャパンナレッジ]]版）、pp. 309-313。。 継体天皇22年（[[528年]]?）11月11日条によると、磐井は[[筑紫国|筑紫]]御井郡（現在の[[福岡県]][[三井郡]]の大部分と[[久留米市]]中央部{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}）において、朝廷から征討のため派遣された[[物部麁鹿火]]の軍と交戦したが、激しい戦いの末に麁鹿火に斬られた。そして同年12月、磐井の子の[[筑紫葛子|筑紫君葛子]]は死罪を免れるため[[糟屋屯倉]]（現・福岡県[[糟屋郡]]・[[福岡市]][[東区 (福岡市)|東区]]{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}）を朝廷に献じたという{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 === 古事記 === 『[[古事記]]』では継体天皇段において、竺紫君石井（磐井に同じ）が天皇の命に従わず無礼が多いため、物部荒甲大連（物部麁鹿火）・[[大伴金村|大伴金村連]]の2人が遣わされて石井を殺した、と事件について簡潔に触れている{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}。 === 風土記 === 『筑後国風土記』逸文（『[[釈日本紀]]』所引）によると、上妻県（かみつやめのあがた：現在の福岡県[[八女郡]]東北部{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}）の役所の南2里（約1キロメートル）に筑紫君磐井の墓があるとする。その墓について詳述した後で古老の伝えとして、雄大迹天皇（継体天皇）の御世に磐井は強い勢力を有して生前に墓を作ったが、俄に官軍が進発し攻めようとしたため、勝ち目のないことを悟って[[豊前国]]上膳県（[[上毛郡]]：現在の福岡県[[築上郡]]南部）へ逃げて身を隠した。そしてこれに怒った官軍は石人・石馬を壊したという{{Sfn|磐井（古代氏族）|2010年}}『[[日本古典文学全集|新編日本古典文学全集]] 5 風土記』小学館、2003年（[[ジャパンナレッジ]]版）、pp. 309-313。。 === その他 === 『[[先代旧事本紀]]』「[[国造本紀]]」伊吉島造（[[壱岐国造]]）条では、継体天皇の時に石井（磐井）に従った新羅の海辺の人を討伐したとする記述がある。 == 墓 == [[File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|200px|thumb|{{center|石馬（岩戸山古墳出土）}}{{small|岩戸山歴史資料館展示。}}]] 磐井の墓は、『筑後国風土記』逸文に詳述されている。これによれば、墓は高さ7丈（約20メートル）、周囲60丈（約180メートル）で、墓域は南辺・北辺各60丈、東辺・西辺各40丈（約120メートル）。また石人・石盾各60枚があり、交互に陣をなして墓の周囲に巡らされた。さらに東北の角には「衙頭（がとう）」と称する別区を設け、衙頭の中には「[[解部]]（ときべ）」という悠然と立つ人物1人と、裸体で大地に伏す「偸人（ぬすびと）」があった。これは生きていた際に盗人が猪を盗んだため、その罪を受けている様子を表したという。その側には「賊物（かすみもの）」と称する盗んだ石猪4頭とともに、石馬3疋、石殿3軒、石蔵2軒があったと伝えている。 以上の記述に見える墓は、現在では[[福岡県]][[八女市]]吉田の'''[[岩戸山古墳]]'''（{{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=岩戸山古墳}}）に比定される{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。この岩戸山古墳は墳丘長135メートルの[[前方後円墳]]で、北部九州では最大、かつ当時の畿内大王墓にも匹敵する規模の古墳になる{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。その築造年代は6世紀前半と推定され『日本書紀』の年代と一致し、また[[石人石馬|石人・石馬]]を含む多くの石製品が出土し、古墳東北隅には別区の存在も確認され、多くの点で『筑後国風土記』逸文とも一致を見せている{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。なお岩戸山古墳北方約1.1キロメートルの正恵大坪遺跡（[[八女郡]][[広川町 (福岡県)|広川町]]古賀）では、上妻郡衙と見られる遺構が見つかっており、これも『筑後国風土記』逸文に見える上妻県役所の後身に対応するものとして注目されている{{Sfn|大塚恵治|2014年|pp=104-107}}。 岩戸山古墳の位置する八女丘陵では、前方後円墳12基（岩戸山古墳含む）・[[装飾古墳]]3基を含む古墳約300基からなる[[八女古墳群]]が分布する{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=9}}。その築造は[[4世紀]]前半から7世紀前半に及び、筑紫君一族の墓に相当すると推定されている{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=9}}。中でも岩戸山古墳の2世代前にあたる[[石人山古墳]]（八女郡広川町一条）は磐井の祖父の墓と推定されるほか{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=14}}、岩戸山古墳次世代の[[乗場古墳]]（八女市吉田）・[[善蔵塚古墳]]（八女郡広川町六田）・[[鶴見山古墳]]（八女市豊福）のいずれかは子の葛子の墓と推定されている{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=24}}。なお、昭和中頃まではそのうちの石人山古墳を磐井の墓とする説が有力視されていた{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=16}}。 == 考証 == {{see also|[[磐井の乱]]}} 磐井の墓と推定される岩戸山古墳は、当時の大王墓にも匹敵する規模であり、また出土した石製品群は北部九州の中でも他古墳を圧倒する数で、北部九州一帯に及んだ磐井の勢力を物語っている{{Sfn|大塚恵治|2014年|pp=104-107}}。その石製品群の分布状況から推測される勢力範囲は、北は[[玄界灘]]、南は[[有明海]]に及んでおり、筑紫君を中心として「筑紫政権」とも呼ぶべき強力な連合政権を形成していたといわれる{{Sfn|角川日本地名大辞典|1988年|p=31}}{{Sfn|大塚恵治|2014年|pp=104-107}}。 近年では、領域の中でも特に有明海沿岸地域が5世紀後半から6世紀初頭にかけての対朝鮮交渉の中心地であったことから、その対朝鮮の外交権を巡って磐井とヤマト王権側との間に対立が生じたとする説が挙げられている{{Sfn|磐井の乱（日本大百科）}}{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2013年|p=196}}。その例として、朝鮮半島の[[栄山江]]流域に分布する[[長鼓墳|前方後円形古墳]]の存在や、栄山江流域・慶南地方に分布する九州系横穴式石室の存在があり、これら九州系豪族が独自に朝鮮半島と密接な交渉を行なった様子が指摘される{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=73-84}}。また日本列島内においても、真の継体天皇陵と目される[[今城塚古墳]]（[[大阪府]][[高槻市]]）を始めとする畿内古墳の石棺部材に[[阿蘇ピンク石]]（馬門ピンク石）が見られることから、九州から西日本・畿内へ文化を波及させるだけの力を有したとされる{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=60-72}}（阿蘇ピンク石の畿内流入は530年頃で終息{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}）。これらから、畿内側が看過できないまで九州勢力が成長していたことが乱の背景になったと考えられている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=73-84}}（詳しくは「[[磐井の乱]]」参照）。 文献の記述に関しては、『筑後国風土記』逸文に記される別区での裁判のような様から、磐井が朝廷において[[解部]]として訴訟に携わったことを表すとする説がある{{Sfn|筑紫磐井（国史）}}。また『日本書紀』における近江毛野との会話からも、やはり磐井が若い頃に王権に出仕した可能性が指摘される{{Sfn|柳沢一男|2014年|pp=60-72}}。ただしこの近江毛野の伝承に関しては、磐井の乱における毛野の動向が記されないことから、磐井の乱と近江毛野の任那派遣とは元々別伝承であったと見られている{{Sfn|水谷千秋|2015年}}。 なお、史書では子の筑紫君葛子の後も[[7世紀]]末まで筑紫君（筑紫氏）一族の名が見られ、その活躍が認められている{{Sfn|筑紫君葛子（古代氏族）|2010年}}{{Sfn|岩戸山歴史資料館|2009年|p=13}}。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} '''注釈''' {{reflist|group="注"}} '''出典''' {{reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * 百科事典 {{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Chikushi no Kimi Iwai}} {{citation needed|Tsunokimi Iwai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}} {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukushi no Iwai}} {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:Q11]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwatoyama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[Category:Q11]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwatoyama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|qq=}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimana|qq=}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Silla|qq=}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Arakabi|qq=}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|qq=}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yame, Fukuoka|qq=}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwatoyama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Iwai]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|qq=}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yame, Fukuoka|qq=}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Tsunokimi Iwai]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwato-yama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojiki|qq=}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Shoki|qq=}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|qq=}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mimana|qq=}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Silla|qq=}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|ja|物部麁鹿火|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mononobe no Arakabi|qq=}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|qq=}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} in {{ill|Yame, Fukuoka|ja|八女市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yame, Fukuoka|qq=}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]]

While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|qq=}} led an army of 60,000 across to [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] to restore lands seized by [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|qq=}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in [[:en:Yame|Yame, Fukuoka]]. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]]

{{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwato-yama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. In the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], his name is written as "Iwai"; in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|qq=}} led an army of 60,000 across to [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] to restore lands seized by [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|qq=}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in [[:en:Yame|Yame, Fukuoka]]. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:Ancient Japanese figures]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:古墳時代以前に戦死した人物]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:筑紫君磐井]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]]

This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|qq=}} led an army of 60,000 across to [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] to restore lands seized by [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|qq=}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in [[:en:Yame|Yame, Fukuoka]]. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun period people]] [[Category:People who died in the Kofun period]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]]}} [[sn:Tsukushi no Iwai]] [[sn:Chikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[sn:hikushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:古墳時代以前に戦死した人物]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:筑紫君磐井]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]]
