His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. In the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], his name is written as "Iwai"; in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] to restore lands seized by [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Kasaya Tonogura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in [[:en:Yame|Yame, Fukuoka]]. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{wikidata link|Q11602917}} [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:People Who Died in Battle Before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]]

[[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{Infobox person|death_date={{death date|0528|11|11}}|death_place={{ill|Mii district|WD=Q11071138}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|title={{ill|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|WD=Q108487213}}|children={{ill|Q20041449|WD=Q20041449|1=Tsukushi no Kuzuko}}|native_name=筑紫 磐井|resting_place={{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|WD=Q11474582}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602917]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi no Iwai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwato-yama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]], some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Iwai Rebellion]], as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Iwai Rebellion]]}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Kasaya Tonogura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in [[:en:Yame|Yame, Fukuoka]]. ([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] to restore lands seized by [[:en:Silla|Silla]]. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under [[:en:Mononobe no Arakabi|Mononobe no Arakabi]] and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Kasaya Tonogura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] in [[:en:Yame|Yame, Fukuoka]]. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:Iwai Rebellion|Iwai Rebellion]] * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{wikidata link|Q11602917}} [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:People Who Died in Battle Before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
[[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{Infobox person|death_date={{death date|0528|11|11}}|death_place={{ill|Mii district|WD=Q11071138}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|title={{ill|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|WD=Q108487213}}|children={{ill|Q20041449|WD=Q20041449|1=Tsukushi no Kuzuko}}|native_name=筑紫 磐井|resting_place={{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|WD=Q11474582}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11602917]] Wikidata name: Tsukushi no Iwai [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwato-yama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}}, some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}}, as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}}}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Kasaya Tonogura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} in {{ill|Yame|en|Yame|WD=Q850492|lt=Yame, Fukuoka}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}} * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{wikidata link|Q11602917}} [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:People Who Died in Battle Before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]]

While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}}, some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}}, as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}}}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Kasaya Tonogura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} in {{ill|Yame|en|Yame|WD=Q850492|lt=Yame, Fukuoka}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}} * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{wikidata link|Q11602917}} [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:People Who Died in Battle Before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]]

revision.text:
[[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]][[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{Infobox person|death_date={{death date|0528|11|11}}|death_place={{ill|Mii district|WD=Q11071138}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|title={{ill|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|WD=Q108487213}}|children={{ill|Q20041449|WD=Q20041449|1=Tsukushi no Kuzuko}}|native_name=筑紫 磐井|resting_place={{ill|Iwatoyama Kofun|WD=Q11474582}}}} {{Infobox noble | Name = Iwai | image = | Era = [[Kofun period]] (Late) | Birth Date = Unknown | Death Date = 22nd year of Emperor Keitai (528? ) | Other Names = Chikushi Iwai, Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai, Tsukushi Kimi Iwai | Grave = [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] in [[Yame, Fukuoka|Yame]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]] ({{Coord|33|13|47.49|N|130|33|9.77|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline|name=Iwatoyama Kofun}}) | Rank = [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]] (kabane) | Clan = Possibly [[Tsukushi Kuni no Miyatsuko|Tsukushi no Kuni no Miyatsuko]] | Children = [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]] }}[[File:Iwatoyama-kofun enkei.JPG|thumb|300px|right|{{center|[[Iwato-yama Kofun]] (Yame, Fukuoka) and its stone products}}{{small|Believed to be Iwai's tomb, the largest kofun in northern Kyushu.}}]] '''Iwai''' (磐井, dates unknown - 22nd year of Emperor Keitai [528? ]), also known as '''Tsukushi Iwai''' (筑紫 磐井), was a regional chieftain in the early 6th century during the late [[Kofun period]]. His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}}, some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}}, as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion}}}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Kasaya Tonogura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} in {{ill|Yame|en|Yame|WD=Q850492|lt=Yame, Fukuoka}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812|1=Q135550812}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}, some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion|1=Iwai Rebellion}}, as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion|1=Iwai Rebellion}}}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Q24874980|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812|1=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} in {{ill|Yame|en|Yame|WD=Q850492|lt=Yame, Fukuoka|1=Yame}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion|1=Iwai Rebellion}} * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{wikidata link|Q11602917}} [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:People Who Died in Battle Before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:pages]]

His kabane was [[Kimi (kabane)|Kimi]]. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, his name is written as "Iwai"; in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, as "Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai"; and in the fragments of the {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812|1=Q135550812}}, as "Tsukushi Kimi Iwai." While the Nihon Shoki describes him as a Kuni no Miyatsuko of the {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}, some scholars suggest this was a later embellishment.{{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}}{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} Iwai is known for leading the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion|1=Iwai Rebellion}}, as well as being one of the rare figures from this era whose tomb has been conclusively identified. == Records == {{Location map+|Japan Northern Kyushu|width=250|float=right|caption={{center|Locations related to the {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion|1=Iwai Rebellion}}}}|places= {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=13|lat_sec=47.49|lon_deg=130|lon_min=33|lon_sec=9.77|position=bottom|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Chikugo Kami-tsuyame
([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変). This shifts the events of 527-528 to 530-531. {{Harv|Iwai Rebellion (Ancient History)|2006}} that {{ill|Omi no Miko|ja|近江毛野|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omi no Miko|1=Oumi no Kena|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8011171}} led an army of 60,000 across to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} to restore lands seized by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. Iwai, a Tsukushi chieftain, reportedly accepted bribes from Silla to block this mission and gained control over trade routes.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} The rebellion escalated, but by the 22nd year (528?), Iwai was defeated by the imperial army under {{ill|Mononobe no Arakabi|en|Mononobe no Arakabi|WD=Q1114837|lt=Mononobe no Arakabi|1=Mononobe no Arakabi}} and killed in battle. His son, [[Tsukushi no Katsurako]], surrendered by offering the {{ill|Kasaya Tonogura|ja|糟屋屯倉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasaya Tonogura|1=Q24874980|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24874980}} to the imperial court.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} === Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki === The {{ill|Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja|筑後国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chikugo no Kuni Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135550812|1=Q135550812}} describes Iwai's Kofun and records that he constructed it during his lifetime but abandoned it after losing his rebellion.{{Harv|Iwai (Ancient Clans)|2010}} == Tomb == {{Gallery |File:岩戸山古墳 石馬.jpg|Stone horse from Iwatoyama Kofun (on display at the Iwatoyama Historical Museum). }} The {{ill|Iwato-yama Kofun|ja|岩戸山古墳|1=Iwato-yama Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwatoyama Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwato-yama Kofun|qq=}}, identified as Iwai's tomb, is a {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} in {{ill|Yame|en|Yame|WD=Q850492|lt=Yame, Fukuoka|1=Yame}}. The largest kofun in northern Kyushu, it dates to the early 6th century.{{Harv|Iwatoyama Historical Museum|2009}} == Legacy == Iwai is considered a pivotal figure symbolizing early resistance to Yamato rule, with his tomb illustrating the wealth and power he wielded over northern Kyushu.{{Harv|Mizutani Chikashi|2013}} == Notes == {{notelist}} == References == {{reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Iwai Rebellion|en|Iwai Rebellion|WD=Q3138004|lt=Iwai Rebellion|1=Iwai Rebellion}} * [[Iwato-yama Kofun]] {{translated page|ja|磐井 (古代豪族)|version=101381485|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:磐井 (古代豪族)]] [[zh:筑紫磐井]] {{wikidata link|Q11602917}} [[Category:528 deaths]] [[Category:5th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:6th-century Japanese people]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:People Who Died in Battle Before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Tsukushi no Kimi Iwai]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]]

revision.comment: renaming these to the better title

([[Iwato-yama Kofun]])}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=19|lat_sec=9.43|lon_deg=130|lon_min=30|lon_sec=30.14|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Tsukushi [[Mii District, Fukuoka|Mii District]]}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=37|lat_sec=3.89|lon_deg=130|lon_min=25|lon_sec=2.61|position=left|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Kasaya Tonogura}} {{Location map~|Japan Northern Kyushu|lat_deg=33|lat_min=34|lat_sec=42.27|lon_deg=131|lon_min=9|lon_sec=50.81|position=right|mark=Red pog.svg|marksize=10|label=Bungo Kamitsuge}} }} === Nihon Shoki === The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} states in the 21st year of Emperor Keitai (527?)The chronology of Emperor Keitai's later years may be three years earlier than recorded due to discrepancies with the Baekje Chronicles (辛亥の変).
